body_txt: The Mafia .... BUT LATIN AMERICAN! An umbrella term for illegal drug trafficking, production, and manufacturing enterprises based in Latin America , primarily Mexico . Not to be confused with literal cartels in the sense of small groups of large companies conspiring to manipulate the market , though that's the source for their name, since each group tries to corner the drug market by violence. In its current form, the illegal drug trade began to develop in The '70s and '80s with the rise of cocaine. One of the most infamous cartels was the Medellin Cartel and its leader Pablo Escobar , who ran most of the cocaine trade in the Americas during the '80s and '90s until he was taken down by the Colombian Search Bloc with the assistance of the United States . Escobar's power and reach were so big during his heyday that he was (and still is) referred to as the "world's greatest outlaw." Additionally, the financial magazine Forbes described him as the "world's richest criminal." Even today, when most people think of Colombia , they think "Cocaine Land" (or should we say, ''País de la Cocaína?" Because "cocaína" is a really common word when discussing these people). As of today, Mexican drug cartels, primarily the Gulf Cartel, the Sinaloa Cartel, and the CJNG, largely control transnational cocaine trafficking, including coca plantations in Colombia. They are also responsible for trafficking other drugs, such as heroin and fentanyl, with the latter causing one of the largest public health crises in contemporary U.S. history. The cartel was a popular villain in fiction during the '80s, when the drug trade made the headlines big time and influenced works like Scarface (1983) and Miami Vice , while also influencing the public perception of drug lords and the cocaine cowboys from that era. To this day, in the collective imagination, it's difficult not to think of the drug lord without thinking of sophisticated assault rifles, cocaine, cowboy hats, brown loafers, mustaches or beards . Certainly, the trope took a Darker and Edgier turn in the 21st century with the Mexican drug war since 2006 note One of the most lethal and violent armed conflicts on the planet and works like Breaking Bad and Sicario , note Works that served as the Trope Codifier for more contemporary iterations of the cartel which saw the rise of a new breed of brutal drug cartels that became infamous for their graphic executions, Cold-Blooded Torture , and use of propaganda to recruit new sicarios and scare their enemies. As expected, these more recent works focus on cartels operating in eerie, desolate locations in the Southwest , portraying them as serious national security threats and even drifting into Terrorists Without a Cause territory. So expect to see these organizations remain popular antagonists for the foreseeable future. For their adversaries and affiliates, see The Mafia , The Mafiya , Kosher Nostra , Yakuza , The Triads and the Tongs , and Gangbangers . See also The Syndicate .
